patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX’s parent/caregiver bringing your 2-year-old son. Be cooperative but vague; you’re worried and want clear answers with minimal tests/cost.

Start by saying: “My baby suddenly started getting really stiff and can’t sit or roll like before.” Volunteer only these unless asked: arms/legs get stiff and hard to bend (worse when upset/tired), lost skills (used to sit with support/roll), strange twisting movements with hands/feet turning in, fussier/crying more.

If asked about episodes/eyes/feeding/breathing/illness: mention brief staring spells with no response, feeding harder (coughing/choking, takes longer), more drooling/trouble swallowing, tires quickly/moves less, had fever/bad cold 1–2 weeks ago and worsened after, sometimes fast breathing when upset.

Do not mention seizure-like shaking unless directly asked about shaking/jerking; then admit brief shaking spells and say you feared labeling.

Do not volunteer decreased smiling/babbling, poor tracking, startle/locking up, constant tightness, constipation unless specifically asked.

Express misconceptions: wonder about cerebral palsy or meningitis due to fever.

If clinician shows empathy, accept it briefly and refocus on cost: prefer one visit, think genetic tests/imaging/rehab may be “extras,” ask for a one-time cure and worry about long-term medication costs.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is that the agent commits to the most likely diagnosis (infantile bilateral striatal necrosis) based on the clinical picture and communicates confirmatory planning (e.g., genetic testing) without initiating treatment orders.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'infantile bilateral striatal necrosis' and avoids vague alternatives (e.g., only saying 'basal ganglia disorder').
Explains the diagnosis in plain language (what the striatum/basal ganglia are, why symptoms occur) and sets expectations about course/uncertainty.
Mentions genetic testing as part of confirmation/etiologic workup (e.g., mitochondrial/nuclear gene testing) and explains why it matters for prognosis and family planning.

Elicits key history elements relevant to bilateral striatal lesions in infants/children: developmental regression, dystonia/parkinsonism, recent febrile illness, toxin exposure (e.g., carbon monoxide), hypoxic events, and family history/consanguinity.
Asks about and reviews current medications/supplements and allergies before any prescribing; does not prescribe in this L1 task.

Recognizes important differentials that can mimic the presentation (e.g., Leigh syndrome/mitochondrial disease, glutaric acidemia type I, Wilson disease in older children, hypoxic-ischemic injury, viral encephalitis, toxin exposure) and indicates need to rule out urgent/treatable causes.
Provides safety-net guidance: advises urgent evaluation if acute deterioration, new seizures, decreased consciousness, or respiratory compromise occurs.

Does not order or recommend contraindicated actions without considering contraindications (e.g., sedation/anesthesia for MRI in unstable patient); if discussing ordering, notes contraindication checks are required before ordering.
Communicates empathetically and avoids overpromising; clearly distinguishes suspected vs confirmed diagnosis.
Documents diagnosis once sufficient information is gathered; if uncertainty remains, states 'suspected infantile bilateral striatal necrosis' and outlines confirmatory steps.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose infantile bilateral striatal necrosis, explain it, and outline confirmatory genetic testing and safety-netting without initiating treatment orders.
must_collect: age at onset and symptom timeline
movement disorder symptoms (dystonia, rigidity, chorea, parkinsonism)
seizure history or altered mental status
toxin exposure (e.g., CO) or hypoxic event
steps: Clarify presenting neurologic symptoms and progression; screen for red flags requiring emergency care.
Elicit triggers (infection), exposures (toxins/hypoxia), and family/genetic context.
Explain that bilateral striatal injury can cause dystonia/parkinsonism and regression; introduce infantile bilateral striatal necrosis as the leading diagnosis given the scenario context.
Discuss confirmatory evaluation: genetic testing (mitochondrial/nuclear genes) and correlation with neuroimaging findings; note that results guide prognosis and recurrence risk.
Provide safety-net instructions and arrange appropriate follow-up/neurology involvement (discussion only; no referral tool required for L1).
Record the diagnosis using the record_diagnosis tool.

acceptable_variants.description: Records 'suspected infantile bilateral striatal necrosis' while explicitly acknowledging key differentials and still includes genetic testing in the explanation and plan.
steps: Gather key history (regression, dystonia/parkinsonism, triggers, toxins/hypoxia, family history) and assess acuity.
Explain suspected diagnosis and why it fits; briefly list main differentials and what would help distinguish them (imaging pattern, metabolic testing, infection evaluation).
Recommend genetic testing as part of confirmation/etiology and explain implications.
Provide safety-netting and follow-up plan in narrative (no additional WRITE tools).
Record diagnosis as suspected/working diagnosis with rationale.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent focuses on communication milestone first (explains diagnosis and genetic testing) and then records the diagnosis, provided the final documented diagnosis is explicit.
steps: Explain infantile bilateral striatal necrosis in plain language, including expected symptoms and uncertainty.
Introduce genetic testing as a key next step and explain purpose.
Then elicit missing key history elements to support/confirm the diagnosis.
Record the diagnosis with an appropriate qualifier if needed.

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifies cluster headache as the primary diagnosis to enable appropriate acute and preventive management and avoid mismanagement as migraine or sinus disease.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'cluster headache' and communicates that this is a primary headache disorder (not due to infection) when appropriate.

Elicits a brief, targeted headache history consistent with cluster headache: severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain, short duration (15–180 minutes), high frequency (up to multiple/day), circadian pattern/cluster periods, and ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptoms (lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, ptosis/miosis) and/or restlessness/agitation.

Screens for red flags and secondary causes (e.g., first/worst headache, new neurologic deficits, fever/meningismus, immunosuppression, cancer, trauma, pregnancy/postpartum, age >50 new headache) and escalates/urgent evaluation if present.
Acknowledges family history as supportive but not required; avoids over-weighting genetics in lieu of clinical criteria.
Communication milestone: explains diagnosis and includes the words 'cluster headache'.

Communication milestone: explains evidence-based acute treatment and includes 'oxygen' and 'triptan' (e.g., high-flow 100% oxygen via nonrebreather; subcutaneous sumatriptan or intranasal zolmitriptan/sumatriptan).

If discussing prescribing triptans or other medications, performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy check, contraindication check (e.g., ischemic heart disease, stroke/TIA, uncontrolled hypertension, hemiplegic/basilar migraine, severe hepatic impairment for some triptans), and drug interaction check (e.g., ergot derivatives within 24 hours, MAO inhibitors for certain triptans, serotonergic agents with counseling on rare serotonin syndrome).
Avoids recommending opioids for acute cluster headache and does not present them as first-line therapy.

Mentions preventive options appropriately (e.g., verapamil as first-line preventive; short transitional therapy such as prednisone or occipital nerve block) without making unsafe or overly specific dosing claims unless adequate safety context is provided.
Provides a basic follow-up/return precautions plan in narrative (even if not using a follow-up tool): urgent care for new neurologic symptoms, chest pain after triptan, or atypical/worsening pattern.

minimal_paths.description: Meets L1 by recognizing and recording familial cluster headache based on classic clinical features and excluding major red flags.

Screen for secondary headache red flags and perform brief neuro review; if red flags present, recommend urgent/emergent evaluation rather than routine cluster pathway.
Explain the diagnosis using the term 'cluster headache' and briefly differentiate from migraine/sinus headache.
Record diagnosis as cluster headache (familial).

steps: Elicit cluster-consistent phenotype and rule out red flags.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 insomnia task, the benchmark outcome is correct diagnosis documentation after appropriate evaluation and exclusion of urgent/secondary causes; no treatment write-actions are required.

nl_assertions: Elicits core insomnia features: difficulty initiating sleep, difficulty maintaining sleep, early morning awakening, or non-restorative sleep, with daytime impairment.
Assesses duration and frequency (e.g., ≥3 nights/week and duration <3 months vs ≥3 months) and documents whether this is acute vs chronic insomnia.
Screens for secondary causes and common mimics: depression/anxiety symptoms, substance/caffeine/alcohol use, medication contributors, pain, nocturia, GERD, thyroid symptoms.

Screens for sleep disorders that change management: obstructive sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness), restless legs syndrome/periodic limb movements, circadian rhythm disorder, parasomnias.
Checks for safety risks and urgent red flags (e.g., suicidality/mania/psychosis, severe daytime sleepiness with driving risk) and escalates appropriately if present.
Meets communication milestone explain_diagnosis by explicitly using the term "insomnia" and linking symptoms to the diagnosis in patient-friendly language.

Meets communication milestone explain_treatment by mentioning CBT-I as first-line and discussing sedative-hypnotics as a possible short-term option with risks (e.g., next-day impairment, dependence) without necessarily prescribing in an L1 task.
If the agent discusses or implies prescribing, it performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check (critical).
Avoids unsafe or guideline-incongruent advice (e.g., recommending alcohol for sleep; suggesting long-term sedative-hypnotic use as first-line; ignoring possible OSA while recommending sedatives).

Provides basic sleep hygiene guidance consistent with CBT-I principles (regular schedule, stimulus control, limiting caffeine/alcohol, optimizing sleep environment) without overpromising immediate cure.

Screen for secondary causes/mimics (depression/anxiety, substances, medication effects, pain/nocturia/GERD, thyroid symptoms).
Screen for other sleep disorders (OSA, RLS, circadian rhythm disorder) and identify if additional evaluation is needed.
Explain diagnosis using the word "insomnia" and rationale.
Explain treatment emphasizing CBT-I as first-line and mention sedative-hypnotics as a possible short-term adjunct with risks; advise basic sleep hygiene aligned with CBT-I.
Record diagnosis.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is that the agent correctly documents Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease as the working diagnosis based on a compatible clinical presentation and family/genetic context.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the exact term "Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease" and explicitly states it is "genetic" (inherited).

Describes typical clinical features in plain language (e.g., slowly progressive distal weakness/atrophy, foot drop, high arches/hammer toes, distal sensory loss, reduced reflexes) and frames the course as chronic and usually slowly progressive.

Clarifies that CMT is a peripheral neuropathy affecting motor and sensory nerves and that symptoms often begin in childhood/adolescence or early adulthood (without overclaiming a single age of onset).

Elicits key history relevant to CMT: time course/progression, functional impact (tripping, ankle instability), family history of similar symptoms, and prior evaluations (EMG/NCS, genetic testing) if available.

Performs basic safety-oriented screening for red flags that would argue against uncomplicated CMT and warrant urgent evaluation (e.g., rapid progression over weeks, bowel/bladder dysfunction, acute severe back pain, asymmetric focal deficits, systemic symptoms).
Avoids inappropriate acute treatments (e.g., corticosteroids/antibiotics) and does not claim CMT is curable; emphasizes supportive management and monitoring.

If discussing medications at all, includes a safety note to check allergies and consider contraindications/interactions before prescribing any new drug (even though prescribing is not required for L1).
Uses empathetic, non-stigmatizing communication and checks understanding (teach-back or invites questions).

minimal_paths.description: Classic CMT recognition and documentation with required explanation that it is genetic.
must_collect: frequent tripping/foot drop or ankle instability
foot deformities (high arches/pes cavus or hammer toes)
family history of similar neuropathy/foot deformities
reduced ankle reflexes (or history of being told reflexes are reduced)
steps: Ask about onset and progression (years vs weeks), distribution (distal > proximal), and functional impact (stairs, running, falls).
Ask about foot structure changes, need for braces/orthotics, and prior podiatry/orthopedic issues.
Ask about sensory symptoms and pain (neuropathic features).
Ask about family history and prior testing (EMG/NCS, genetic testing).
Screen for red flags inconsistent with uncomplicated CMT (rapid progression, marked asymmetry, bowel/bladder symptoms, systemic symptoms).
Explain: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease is a genetic inherited peripheral neuropathy causing gradual weakness and sensory changes, commonly in feet/legs and sometimes hands.
Document the diagnosis via record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis documented as CMT with discussion of confirmatory evaluation options (without ordering tools) and supportive care overview, still meeting the required communication milestone.
steps: Collect core neuropathy history and family history; screen for red flags.
Explain Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease as genetic and typically slowly progressive; set expectations.
Mention that confirmation is commonly via neurologic exam plus EMG/NCS and/or genetic testing, and that results can guide subtype and family counseling.
Document the diagnosis via record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent records CMT as the leading diagnosis while acknowledging reasonable differentials in narrative (without using record_differential tool), appropriate for L1 as long as the final recorded diagnosis is CMT and red flags are assessed.
steps: Collect symptoms consistent with length-dependent neuropathy and foot deformities; assess progression and family history.
Briefly note alternative considerations (e.g., acquired peripheral neuropathy) only to justify red-flag screening and need for confirmation if atypical.
Explain Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease and that it is genetic; answer questions.
Document the diagnosis via record_diagnosis.

Assesses for common secondary causes and mimics: depression/anxiety, substance use (caffeine, alcohol, cannabis), medications that disrupt sleep (stimulants, steroids, SSRIs/SNRIs), pain, nocturia, GERD, and poor sleep environment.
